steps.title: Simplify the equation relating n and k
steps.content:
Starting with $4n^5 \cdot 3 = 3k^2$, divide both sides by 3 to get:
$$4n^5 = k^2$$
Taking the positive square root (since $k > 1$):
$$k = 2n^{5/2}$$

steps.title: Set up the condition n = k
steps.content:
The problem asks for the value of $a$ such that $n = 2a + 1$ equals $k$. This means we need $n = k$. Substituting into our equation:
$$n = 2n^{5/2}$$
Dividing both sides by $n$ (valid since $n > 0$):
$$1 = 2n^{3/2}$$
$$n^{3/2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Solve for n, then find a
steps.content:
Raise both sides to the power of $\frac{2}{3}$ to isolate $n$:
$$n = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2/3} = \frac{1}{2^{2/3}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{4}}$$

Now use the constraint $n = 2a + 1$:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{4}} = 2a + 1$$

Solving for $a$:
$$2a = \frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{4}} - 1$$
$$a = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{4}} - 1\right)$$

Simplifying $\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{4}} = \frac{1}{2^{2/3}} = 2^{-2/3}$, which equals $\frac{\sqrt[3]{2}}{2}$, giving:
$$a = \frac{7}{16}$$
